nodes.description: Vibrio cholerae produces a toxin that activates adenylate cyclase in intestinal epithelial cells, leading to increased cyclic AMP (cAMP) levels.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanism of action of cholera toxin & other toxins.
evidence.snippet: Vibrio cholerae produce a variety of extracellular products that have deleterious effects on eukaryotic cells... CT acts by activation of adenylate cyclase-cAMP system located at the basolateral membrane of intestinal epithelial cells.

evidence.reference_title: Adenylyl Cyclase 6 Expression Is Essential for Cholera Toxin-Induced Diarrhea.
evidence.snippet: Cholera toxin (CT)-induced diarrhea is mediated by cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP)-mediated active Cl- secretion via the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR).

evidence.reference_title: Action of Escherichia coli enterotoxin: adenylate cyclase behavior of intestinal epithelial cells in culture.
evidence.snippet: Comparable results were also obtained when cholera toxin was used. The degree of enzyme stimulation was proportional to the concentration of enterotoxin.

evidence.reference_title: Mucosal immune responses to intestinal bacterial pathogens.
evidence.snippet: V cholerae, the representative noninvasive pathogen, has fimbrial adhesins that mediate attachment and colonization... organisms secrete cholera toxin (CT), a potent enterotoxin that induces a voluminous diarrhea via adenylate cyclase-dependent chloride secretion.

nodes.description: Cholera toxin B subunit (CTB) binds to GM1 ganglioside and alternative fucosylated glycan receptors (e.g., Lewis X) on the apical surface of intestinal epithelial cells, initiating receptor-mediated endocytosis and retrograde trafficking to the endoplasmic reticulum.

evidence.reference_title: Determinants of severe dehydration from diarrheal disease at hospital presentation: Evidence from 22 years of admissions in Bangladesh.
evidence.snippet: Vibrio cholerae was the most common pathogen isolated (12,405 patients; 22%), and had the strongest association with SD (AOR 4.77; 95% CI: 4.41-5.51).

evidence.reference_title: Acidosis in a patient with cholera: a need to redefine concepts.
evidence.snippet: A patient presented with cholera and a severe degree of ECF volume contraction. Despite large losses of bicarbonate (HCO3-)-containing diarrhoeal fluid, laboratory acid-base values were remarkably close to normal.

evidence.reference_title: Cholera Presenting With Hyperkalemia, Rhabdomyolysis, and Acute Renal Failure.
evidence.snippet: It was later confirmed that he was suffering from cholera. On presentation, he was hyperkalemic with ECG changes and soon went into a hypovolemic shock.

nodes.description: The A1 enzymatic subunit of cholera toxin is unfolded by protein disulfide isomerase in the ER and hijacks the ER-associated degradation (ERAD) machinery to retro-translocate into the cytosol, where it refolds to become enzymatically active.
Cytosolic CTA1 catalyzes ADP-ribosylation of the Gsα subunit (GNAS) using NAD+ as substrate, locking adenylyl cyclase in a constitutively active state.

biological_processes: adenylate cyclase-activating G protein-coupled receptor signaling pathway

nodes.description: Elevated cAMP levels cause cells to secrete large amounts of chloride ions into the intestinal lumen, followed by water and other electrolytes.

evidence.reference_title: Cholera: pathophysiology and emerging therapeutic targets.
evidence.snippet: Increased intracellular cAMP in human intestinal epithelial cells accounts for pathogenesis of profuse diarrhea and severe fluid loss in cholera

evidence.reference_title: Adenylyl Cyclase 6 Expression Is Essential for Cholera Toxin-Induced Diarrhea.
evidence.snippet: Cholera toxin (CT)-induced diarrhea is mediated by cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP)-mediated active Cl- secretion via the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR).

evidence.reference_title: Cholera toxin effects on fluid secretion, adenylate cyclase, and cyclic AMP in porcine small intestine.
evidence.snippet: Cholera toxin causes net secretion of fluid into the small intestine of weanling pigs, and secretory rates are dependent on the dose of the toxin placed in intestinal loops.
Intestinal infection with Vibrio cholerae results in the loss of large volumes of watery stool, leading to severe and rapidly progressing dehydration and shock.

evidence.reference_title: Acidosis in a patient with cholera: a need to redefine concepts.
evidence.snippet: A patient presented with cholera and a severe degree of ECF volume contraction. Despite large losses of bicarbonate (HCO3-)-containing diarrhoeal fluid, laboratory acid-base values were remarkably close to normal.

evidence.reference_title: Determinants of severe dehydration from diarrheal disease at hospital presentation: Evidence from 22 years of admissions in Bangladesh.
evidence.snippet: To take advantage of emerging opportunities to reduce morbidity and mortality from diarrheal disease, we need to better understand the determinants of life-threatening severe dehydration (SD) in resource-poor settings.
A 28-year-old female presented with severe dehydration due to acute diarrhea and vomiting... which is typical for cholera and can amount to 1000 cc/hour.

evidence.reference_title: Acute watery diarrhoea cases during cholera outbreak in Syria: a cohort study.
evidence.snippet: The most common complications reported at admission and during hospitalisation included electrolyte imbalance (28.2%), followed by severe dehydration (16.3%).
